questions.question: If a carrier female (X^H X^h) marries a normal male (X^H Y), what percentage of sons will have haemophilia?
The diagram shows a pedigree of X-linked recessive colour blindness. What pattern confirms X-linked recessive inheritance?
questions.diagram_description: Pedigree chart with squares=males, circles=females, shaded=affected, half-shaded=carrier. Generation I: normal parents. Generation II: carrier female, normal male, affected male. Generation III: children of carrier female x normal male show 25% affected males. Key features: no father-to-son transmission of trait, carrier mothers pass to sons, females rarely affected unless homozygous.

questions.question: Philadelphia chromosome is associated with:
In a pedigree, if the trait appears in every generation, it is likely:
Non-disjunction during meiosis I results in:

options.B: Gametes with n+1 and n-1 chromosomes

questions.question: The diagram shows human karyotype of Down syndrome. Which chromosome shows trisomy?
questions.diagram_description: Karyotype image with chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs. Chromosomes 1-22 shown in homologous pairs. Chromosome 21 pair shows THREE chromosomes (trisomy 21) instead of two. Total chromosome number = 47. Chromosomes arranged from largest (1) to smallest (21) with sex chromosomes XX or XY separate. Down syndrome features listed: intellectual disability, flat face, epicanthal folds, Brushfield spots.

questions.question: Cri-du-chat syndrome is caused by:

options.B: Deletion of short arm of chromosome 5

questions.question: A female with haemophilia must have received the X^h gene from:

options.B: Both her haemophiliac father and carrier/affected mother

questions.question: Linked genes do NOT follow Mendel's law of:
Patau syndrome involves trisomy of:
The SRY gene is located on:
The diagram shows crossing over between homologous chromosomes. At what stage does crossing over occur?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram of meiosis I prophase sub-stages: leptotene (chromosomes condense), zygotene (homologues pair/synapse), pachytene (bivalents fully formed, crossing over occurs between non-sister chromatids at chiasmata), diplotene (chiasmata visible), diakinesis (bivalents maximally condensed). Second diagram shows two homologous chromosomes with synaptonemal complex and chiasma points.

questions.question: Jacob syndrome has genotype:
Recombination frequency between two genes determines their:
Haemophilia is rare in females because:

options.B: Females need two defective X chromosome copies to be affected

questions.question: The diagram shows chromosomal structural mutations. Which type involves segment reversal?
questions.diagram_description: Four diagrams of chromosomal structural changes with before/after: (A) Deletion - segment lost, chromosome shorter; (B) Duplication - extra copy of segment, chromosome longer; (C) Inversion - segment detached and reattached in reversed orientation (paracentric if doesn't include centromere, pericentric if includes centromere); (D) Translocation - segment moved to non-homologous chromosome. Each shown with chromosome diagrams.

questions.question: Androgen insensitivity syndrome (AIS) is characterized by:

options.B: Phenotypically female individual with 46 XY genotype due to non-functional androgen receptors

questions.question: Y-linked (holandric) traits are transmitted:

options.B: From father to all sons only

questions.question: In Down syndrome, non-disjunction usually occurs in:

options.B: Oogenesis (maternal non-disjunction, increases with maternal age)

questions.question: Number of Barr bodies in a cell equals:

options.B: Number of X chromosomes minus one

questions.question: The diagram shows a pedigree of autosomal dominant disorder with affected heterozygous parent (Aa) x normal (aa). What proportion of children are affected?
questions.diagram_description: Pedigree showing affected father (Aa, shaded square) married to normal mother (aa, open circle). Punnett square: Aa x aa = 50% Aa (affected) + 50% aa (normal). Children shown: 2 affected, 2 normal. Features of autosomal dominant: every generation affected, father-to-son transmission possible, 50% offspring affected if one parent heterozygous.

questions.question: Thalassaemia is caused by:

options.B: Reduced or absent synthesis of alpha or beta globin chains

questions.question: A carrier female of haemophilia married an affected male. Probability of carrier daughter:
The linkage group number equals:

options.A: Number of chromosomes in diploid cell

questions.question: Edward syndrome (Trisomy 18) features include:

options.B: Severe defects, rocker-bottom feet, clenched fists, very poor prognosis

questions.question: The chromosomal theory of heredity was proposed by:
If a father is colour blind (X^cb Y), his daughters from a normal mother will be:
FISH (Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization) is used to:

options.B: Detect specific chromosomal sequences using fluorescent probes

questions.question: Sickle cell anaemia mutation is:

options.B: Point mutation (GAG to GTG) causing Glu to Val in beta-globin

questions.question: Fragile X syndrome involves:

options.B: Trinucleotide repeat expansion (CGG) in FMR1 gene on X chromosome

questions.question: 1 centimorgan (cM) equals:
In pedigree analysis, father-to-son transmission of a trait rules out:
The diagram shows the pedigree with haemophiliac son from unaffected parents. Which parent is the carrier?
questions.diagram_description: Pedigree: Generation I: normal female x normal male. Generation II: three children: normal daughter, normal son, haemophiliac son (shaded square). Analysis: Father is XY unaffected so cannot carry X^h on Y. Son inherited X from mother and Y from father. Since son is X^h Y, he got X^h from mother. Therefore mother is carrier X^H X^h. Father only contributes Y to son.

questions.question: Triple X syndrome (XXX) in females is:

options.B: Usually fertile females with mild or no symptoms

Which disorder is caused by excess of sex chromosomes (47,XXX)?
